models the answer proved to be a term-grain coefficient relation with
self-describing metadata; for random forests and gradient-boosted
ensembles it is plausibly a node-grain relation of split rules; for future
model classes it is a question each must answer before it can enter SQL
*as SQL*. Posed this way, statistical modeling in databases stops being
only a systems problem — how to compute efficiently near the data — and
becomes also a language problem: which properties of SQL must survive the
encounter, and what relational form statistical objects must take for that
to happen. The five principles and the running example of this paper are
offered as one worked answer and a template for the next ones.

The immediate continuations — alternative engines behind the unchanged
specification, further families and the tree-ensemble relations, and
public release of the extension and its replication material — were
discussed above and are underway; they extend the evidence, while the
proposal this paper stands on is the language.

# Replication material

Everything reported in this paper can be re-executed from two public
repositories whose division of labor is fixed. The **FbSQL repository**
(<https://github.com/dsc-chiba-u/FbSQL>) holds the extension source, the
conformance suite — which contains the running example verbatim, so every
number printed in the *Running Example* section is re-verified by
`make installcheck` — the Docker development environment, and this
manuscript. The **FbSQL-experiments repository**
(<https://github.com/dsc-chiba-u/FbSQL-experiments>) holds the comparative
material: pinned Docker environments for Apache MADlib, PostgresML, and
Spark MLlib, numbered scripts that reproduce the running example on each
system, the R-parity comparison, the per-claim sources for the two
literature-based systems, and the version-controlled comparison data
(`data/related_work.csv`) from which Table 1 and Table 2 of this paper are
generated by script — the paper never edits its tables by hand. All raw
outputs and summary tables are committed, so a re-run surfaces any
deviation as a diff. Scripts resolve repository locations through
environment variables or the sibling-checkout convention, with no
developer-specific paths.

<!-- TODO(replication): archive snapshots of both repositories at Zenodo
     at submission time and insert the DOIs here (fbrglm pattern). -->

# Computational details {.unnumbered}

The reference implementation was developed and tested with PostgreSQL
16.14 [@postgresql], PL/R 8.4.8.6 [@plr], and R 4.2.2 [@rcore], pinned in
the Docker image defined by `docker/Dockerfile` in the FbSQL repository;
no other runtime dependencies exist, and prediction requires no R. The
`pg_regress` conformance suite (eleven test files) is executed inside that
image by GitHub Actions on every commit. The comparison systems ran in
their own pinned Docker environments in the companion repository: Apache
MADlib 1.21.0 on PostgreSQL 11, PostgresML 2.7.12 on its bundled
PostgreSQL 15, and Spark MLlib 3.5.1 in local mode. This manuscript is
rendered from a single R Markdown source in a further pinned image
(`rocker/verse` 4.4.2), with its figures generated by committed R scripts
and its tables generated from the companion repository's data.

<!-- TODO(release sync): once the PGXN release of 0.1.0 is live, update
     the sentence below from "planned" to the released state (pgxn install
     fbsql) and re-check the README wording it references. -->

# Software availability {.unnumbered}

FbSQL is open-source software under the MIT license. The extension, its
conformance suite, and the Docker development environment are available
from the FbSQL repository on GitHub, whose address is given with the
replication material above; at the time of writing the extension installs
from a source checkout via PGXS (`make install`), as described in the
repository README. A release through PGXN, the PostgreSQL
Extension Network, is planned; the release metadata (`META.json`) is
already part of the repository.

# Acknowledgments {.unnumbered}

We thank the developers of PostgreSQL and of PL/R for the infrastructure
on which the reference implementation rests, and the communities behind
the open-source systems compared in this paper — Apache MADlib,
PostgresML, Apache Spark, Apache Hivemall, and H2O — whose released
software and documentation made the comparative evaluation possible. We
thank the Data Science Core, Chiba University, for institutional support,
and the RIKEN TRIP Headquarters Advanced General Intelligence for Science
(AGIS) Program for hosting the Omics AI Research Team during the
development of this work.

     grant numbers (none listed yet), coauthors' institutional
     acknowledgments (Juntendo / Chuo), and any individual reviewers or
     colleagues to be named. -->

# Declarations {.unnumbered}

**Funding** [To be completed at submission.]

**Competing interests** [To be completed at submission.]

**Data availability** All data used in this paper are small synthetic
datasets included in the public repositories: the running-example table
ships inside the extension's conformance suite and as `data/customer.csv`
in the companion repository, and the comparison data is version-controlled
there as `data/related_work.csv`.

**Code availability** The FbSQL extension and the companion experiment
repository are open source under the MIT license; see the replication
material section. [Zenodo DOIs to be added at submission.]

**Authors' contributions** [To be completed at submission.]
